elements.sent-2411-001: Objective: Overhead tasks are a primary inducement to work-related musculoskeletal disorders.
elements.sent-2411-002: Aiming to reduce shoulder physical loads, passive shoulder exoskeletons are increasingly prevalent in the industry due to their lightweight, affordability, and effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, they can only handle specific tasks and struggle to balance compactness with a sufficient range of motion effectively.
elements.sent-2411-004: Method: We proposed a novel passive occupational shoulder exoskeleton designed to handle various overhead tasks at different arm elevation angles, ensuring sufficient ROM while maintaining compactness.
elements.sent-2411-005: By formulating kinematic models and simulations, an ergonomic shoulder structure was developed.
elements.sent-2411-006: Then, we presented a torque generator equipped with an adjustable peak assistive torque angle to switch between low and high assistance phases through a passive clutch mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-007: Ten healthy participants were recruited to validate its functionality by performing the screwing task.
elements.sent-2411-008: Results: Measured range of motion results demonstrated that the exoskeleton can ensure a sufficient ROM in both sagittal (164°) and horizontal (158°) flexion/extension movements.
elements.sent-2411-009: The experimental results of the screwing task showed that the exoskeleton could reduce muscle activation (up to 49.6$\%$), perceived effort and frustration, and provide an improved user experience (scored 79.7 out of 100).
elements.sent-2411-010: Conclusion: These results indicate that the proposed exoskeleton can guarantee natural movements and provide efficient assistance during overhead work, and thus have the potential to reduce the risk of musculoskeletal disorders.
elements.sent-2411-011: Significance: The proposed exoskeleton provides insights into multi-task adaptability and efficient assistance, highlighting the potential for expanding the application of exoskeletons.

elements.sent-2411-014: Shoulder WMSDs have received widespread attention due to their high prevalence (13$\%$ of cases) and prolonged absenteeism (23 days) \cite{wang2017work .}
elements.sent-2411-015: Overhead work often relates to extreme and awkward shoulder postures \cite{sluiter2001criteria , which is recognized as a primary contributor \cite{ding2023lightweight .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Although robots have been involved in many factory tasks, numerous complex activities that demand human cognition or operate in narrow spaces still require workers \cite{huysamen2018evaluation .}
elements.sent-2411-017: Occupational shoulder exoskeleton (OSE) technology is an available solution to reduce the burden on the shoulder \cite{park2021shoulder .}
elements.sent-2411-018: OSEs can be classified into active, semi-active, and passive categories.
elements.sent-2411-019: In general, active OSEs can provide higher, more precise, and versatile assistive torques \cite{zhou2021kinematics, grazi2022kinematics, otten2018evaluation .}
elements.sent-2411-020: However, they have larger volumes and higher maintenance costs \cite{kim2022passive .}
elements.sent-2411-021: As an alternative solution, semi-active OSEs can automatically adjust torque profiles by employing small low-power motors \cite{crea2021occupational , such as the H-PULSE \cite{grazi2022kinematics , \cite{grazi2020design .}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: Compared to active ones, it is more compact and has lower energy consumption while maintaining effectiveness.
elements.sent-2411-023: However, compared to passive ones, the maintenance and manufacturing costs of semi-active OSEs are higher, which would limit their widespread adoption and application.
elements.sent-2411-024: Passive OSEs (POSEs) are characterized by their low cost and lightweight design \cite{pacifico2022exoskeletons , \cite{de2016exoskeletons , which provide arm gravity compensation during overhead works without external power sources.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: Recent studies have demonstrated that wearing POSEs can reduce muscle activation during overhead tasks performed in laboratory and field settings \cite{de2020passive, de2023passive, iranzo2020ergonomics .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Even though POSEs have been commercialized, some pending issues remain, thus impeding user acceptance and assistive efficiency \cite{rossini2021design .}
elements.sent-2411-027: One of the main issues is the insufficient kinematic compatibility between the exoskeleton and the user \cite{naf2018misalignment .}
elements.sent-2411-028: POSEs can be classified based on the presence or absence of an exoskeletal shoulder structure above the human shoulder \cite{kim2022passive .}
elements.sent-2411-029: Exoskeletons with a rigid structure above the human shoulder, such as the ShoulderX \cite{pinho2022shoulder and Airframe \cite{mcfarland2022level , typically feature a degree of freedom (DoF) next to the shoulder to follow shoulder flexion/extension (F/E) movements.}}
elements.sent-2411-030: These exoskeletons are compact and thus are suitable for performing overhead tasks in narrow spaces.
elements.sent-2411-031: However, they may induce collisions between the shoulder and the exoskeleton at high elevation angles, resulting in an insufficient range of motion (ROM).
elements.sent-2411-032: Exoskeletons without a structure above the shoulder, such as the SPM \cite{hunt2017novel and Exo4Work \cite{de2022occupational , avoid collisions at high elevation angles.However, they generally incorporate multi-DoF structures in the back to accommodate shoulder movements, which leads to a larger volume.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: The drawback may result in collisions with the surrounding objects in narrow spaces.
elements.sent-2411-034: In existing exoskeletons, achieving both sufficient ROM and compactness is mutually exclusive.
elements.sent-2411-035: To achieve a balance between them, Junsoo Kim et al. \cite{kim2022passive designed an exoskeleton with tilted shoulder structures for assisting overhead tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-036: However, it still cannot provide a sufficient ROM for horizontal F/E movements, which may induce a decreased user experience.
elements.sent-2411-037: There is an urgent need for an exoskeleton that can simultaneously provide sufficient ROM and compactness.

Angle difference refers to the discrepancy between the PATA and target angle of the task.} \label{fig1}
elements.sent-2411-038: Another main issue is the task-specific or individual-specific design, corresponding to a fixed assistive torque profile and thus a fixed assistive region (i.e. the subset of ROM where the exoskeleton can assist), which may not be optimal for other tasks \cite{maurice2019objective , \cite{hyun2019light .}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Overhead tasks relate to different positions, especially different elevation angles of the shoulder (e.g., screwing task of the automobile manufacturing industry \cite{pinho2022shoulder, yin2020effects, kim2018assessing , aircraft squeeze riveting and sealing tasks \cite{jorgensen2022influence , \cite{jorgensen2022impact , the welding task of shipbuilding \cite{kim2022passive , order picking task \cite{de2020passive and so on).}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: Consequently, various overhead tasks require different optimal torque profiles \cite{grazi2020design , \cite{de2022occupational .}}
elements.sent-2411-041: In addition, the same task may have different target angles for individuals of different heights, which requires providing personalized torque profiles \cite{vazzoler2022analysis .}
elements.sent-2411-042: The torque profile should be adaptive to tasks and subjects, essentially characterized by the amplitude and peak assistive torque angle (PATA).
elements.sent-2411-043: The amplitude of the peak assistive torque can be easily tuned by adjusting the extension length of the spring.
elements.sent-2411-044: For example, the MATE can manually change the peak torque by a knob \cite{pacifico2020experimental , and H-PULSE can adjust the spring length by a spindle drive coupled with a servomotor \cite{grazi2022kinematics , \cite{grazi2020design .}}}
elements.sent-2411-045: The timing of the peak assistive torque ensures that the POSE can provide timely assistance when the torque profile reaches its peak value at specific elevation angles of the shoulder \cite{rossini2021design .}
elements.sent-2411-046: Fig. \ref{fig1 depicts the torque profiles when there is a match or mismatch between the PATA and the target angle of the task in an overhead task with a target angle of 120º.}
elements.sent-2411-047: When there is a match between them, it ensures timely assistance and reduces energy loss, making it a more efficient assistive strategy.
elements.sent-2411-048: However, current POSEs can only change the PATA through reconstruction, which means they cannot provide torque profiles for different tasks or individuals, i.e., insufficient adaptability \cite{hyun2019light , \cite{vazzoler2022analysis .}}
elements.sent-2411-049: In summary, different tasks and individuals should have different PATAs, lacking in current POSEs.
elements.sent-2411-050: Therefore, an adjustable PATA is needed to enhance task and subject adaptability.
elements.sent-2411-051: The object of this study is to develop, implement, and preliminarily evaluate a novel POSE for overhead tasks, named HIT-POSE.
elements.sent-2411-052: Our prototype can provide a sufficient ROM of the human shoulder and possess an adjustable PATA, thus adapting to various overhead tasks and individuals.The major contributions of our work are as follows: (a) We designed a torque generator with adjustable PATA to adapt to different overhead tasks and individuals.
elements.sent-2411-053: To the best of our knowledge, it’s the first torque generator that can provide adjustable PATA to achieve more efficient assistance.
elements.sent-2411-054: Overhead work is defined as working with hands above acromion or over 60° shoulder flexion or abduction \cite{grieve2008overhead .}
elements.sent-2411-055: Therefore, we can define the workspace for overhead tasks as the sagittal flexion of the shoulder with 60° to 168.8° (the maximum shoulder flexion angle for healthy adult male \cite{soucie2011range ), abduction angle of the shoulder greater than 60°, and horizontal flexion of the shoulder with 0° to 162.2° (the maximum shoulder horizontal flexion angle for healthy adult male \cite{kim2022passive ).}}
elements.sent-2411-056: In addition, the total weight of the exoskeleton should be less than 5kg \cite{rossini2021design .}
elements.sent-2411-057: HIT-POSE is designed to compensate for shoulder gravitational torque while providing sufficient ROM for the shoulder, as illustrated in Fig. \ref{fig2 .}
elements.sent-2411-058: The total weight of the device 2.8 kg.
elements.sent-2411-059: It is suitable for people with a back length of 43.6-66.6 cm and a shoulder width of 29.6-45.6 cm, and the volume of our device is 22cm × 48cm × 15cm.
elements.sent-2411-060: HIT-POSE comprises four main modules: (a) the ergonomic shoulder structure, (b) the torque generator with adjustable PATA, (c) the physical user-exoskeleton interface (PUEI), and (d) the size regulation module.
elements.sent-2411-061: We conducted kinematic modeling of the exoskeleton and the user and simulated ROM to select optimal design parameters.

elements.sent-2411-094: The torque generator is composed of a sagittal F/E joint bar, PATA adjustable module, parallel spring group, spring pretension tuning module, carbon fiber shell, and five pulleys with inner bearings (Fig. \ref{fig6a ).}
elements.sent-2411-095: A wire rope of 1mm diameter connects the parallel spring group to pulley 2.
elements.sent-2411-096: The pulleys 3 and 4 can be moved vertically through the guide rail, resulting in the adjustable PATA.
elements.sent-2411-097: The 8-shaped loop with a rotational DoF connects the parallel spring group with the spring pretension tuning module.
elements.sent-2411-098: It ensures that the parallel spring group does not experience axial rotation when screwing the spring pretension tuning module.
elements.sent-2411-099: Three identical springs (i.e., \(L_{s}\)=\(L_{AB}\)=\(L_{CD}\)=\(L_{EF}\)) are installed in the parallel spring group to obtain a high energy storage capability.
elements.sent-2411-100: The assistive principle is to store or release the elastic potential energy of springs as the arm is lowered or raised, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-101: Since the torque generator follows the arm movement via an arm sleeve, it can be considered that the sagittal F/E angle of the exoskeleton (\(\theta_{s,exo}\)) is approximately equal to that of the user (\(\theta_{s,user}\)).

elements.sent-2411-103: Furthermore, \(F_{s}\) can be expressed as: \begin{equation}F_{s}=K\Delta{L}.\label{eq2}\end{equation} where \(K\) is the stiffness of the parallel spring group, which is triple that of a single spring (\(k_{s}\)), i.e., \(K\)=3\(k_{s}\), and \(\Delta{L}\) represents the total extension length of the parallel spring group.

elements.sent-2411-148: Two experimental sessions were conducted to verify the HIT-POSE has (a) a sufficient ROM (session 1), and (b) an ability and adaptability to assist different overhead tasks (session 2).
elements.sent-2411-149: Two separate groups of participants were recruited for this study.
elements.sent-2411-150: Five healthy subjects participated in session 1 (all male, all right-handed, age: 24.1 ± 1.64 years, height: 173.8 ± 3.27 cm, weight: 70 ± 9.06 kg), and ten healthy subjects participated in session 2 (all male, all right-handed, age: 25.1 ± 2.0 years, height: 177 ± 4.3 cm, weight: 68.3 ± 9.3 kg).
elements.sent-2411-151: All participants had no prior experience with the exoskeleton and signed an informed consent before the experiment.
elements.sent-2411-152: The experimental protocol received approval from the Chinese Ethics Committee of Registering Clinical Trials (ChiECRCT20200319).
elements.sent-2411-153: ROM of the right-side shoulder was measured by the motion capture system (100Hz, VICON, Oxford Metrics, Oxford, UK).
elements.sent-2411-154: Muscular activities were monitored using the surface EMG electrodes (1111Hz, Delsys Inc., Natick, MA, USA).
elements.sent-2411-155: Meanwhile, acceleration signals were recorded through an IMU (148Hz, Delsys Inc., Natick, MA, USA).
elements.sent-2411-156: Notably, the motion capture system did not work simultaneously with EMG.
elements.sent-2411-157: Thus, we did not synchronize them through a trigger.

elements.sent-2411-162: In session 1, tasks were performed under two conditions: without the exoskeleton (NO HIT-POSE) and with the exoskeleton (HIT-POSE).

elements.sent-2411-208: HIT-POSE is equipped with an ergonomic shoulder structure to ensure sufficient ROM for the user.
elements.sent-2411-209: This implies favorable kinematics compatibility, which has a profound impact on the exoskeleton’s usability and user acceptance.
elements.sent-2411-210: Typically, some passive shoulder exoskeletons integrate a multi-DoF chain or simplified two-link hinge to increase the ROM, such as the SPM \cite{hunt2017novel and HUST-EV \cite{du2022development .}}
elements.sent-2411-211: However, they may collide with nearby objects easily due to the large volume and protrusions.
elements.sent-2411-212: In addition, the design parameters were fixed, thus lacking reconfigurability.
elements.sent-2411-213: This reduces the adaptability and increases the manufacturing costs of the exoskeleton.
elements.sent-2411-214: To ensure sufficient ROM while compactness, we extracted three parameters (\(\phi\), \(d_{v}\), \(d_{b}\)) to design the shoulder structure, and two of them (\(d_{v}\) and \(d_{b}\)) an be adjusted without reconstructing the exoskeleton.
elements.sent-2411-215: A suitable set of parameters (\(\phi\)=15°, \(d_{v}\)=80mm, \(d_{b}\)=10mm) was selected through kinematic modeling and ROM simulations.
elements.sent-2411-216: Constrained but sufficient ROM was guaranteed in both sagittal and horizontal F/E for performing overhead work, as shown in Table \ref{tab5 , while the structure with a relatively small volume (22cm × 48cm × 15cm) is designed to reduce the possibility of collision.}
elements.sent-2411-217: Since volume information for other devices was not found, no comparison was made.
elements.sent-2411-218: However, our exoskeleton volume is visually more compact than its counterparts.
elements.sent-2411-219: The discrepancies between the ROM in simulations and experiments may be due to the omission of friction and contact forces in the simulations.
elements.sent-2411-220: We had a trade-off for the shoulder movements, limiting the abduction/adduction movements of the shoulder, which are not the focus of overhead work.
elements.sent-2411-221: Through the coordination of sagittal and horizontal F/E joints, the exoskeleton can achieve abduction/adduction movements in the anterior space of the body, but there are limitations on movements in the posterior area of the body, such as extending the elbow diagonally backward and outward.
elements.sent-2411-222: This method meets the design requirements of the overhead work, and similar degree of freedom configuration strategies can be found on the ShoulderX \cite{ojelade2023three and Airframe \cite{mcfarland2022level .}}
elements.sent-2411-223: As shown in Fig. 13, wearing the exoskeleton reduced hand workspace, likely due to a corresponding reduction in shoulder ROM.
elements.subsection-2411-015: Comparison of Adaptability with Existing Exoskeletons
elements.sent-2411-224: Various overhead tasks and individuals usually have different PATAs and thus different optimal torque profiles \cite{maurice2019objective , \cite{hyun2019light .}}
elements.sent-2411-225: Consequently, the task and individual adaptability of the exoskeleton are crucial, which essentially involves adjustable PATA and peak torque.
elements.sent-2411-226: Existing exoskeletons have made considerable efforts in adjusting the assistive levels with different peak torque.
elements.sent-2411-227: Several exoskeletons employ a manual knob or an electrical component to adjust the assistive level, such as the Shoulder X \cite{pinho2022shoulder and H-PULSE \cite{grazi2022kinematics, grazi2020design , other devices require to replace the spring or other parts affecting the spring length.}}
elements.sent-2411-228: However, existing exoskeletons can only adjust the amplitude of peak torque, thus just adapting to a specific task.
elements.sent-2411-229: Employing torque profiles with a fixed PATA to cope with all different tasks and individuals is not an energy-efficient way and limits the adaptability of the torque generator \cite{hyun2019light , \cite{vazzoler2022analysis .}}
elements.sent-2411-230: For such exoskeletons, if we need to adjust the PATA, we can only change it by reconstructing the exoskeleton.
elements.sent-2411-231: To enhance the task adaptability and personalized customization capability of the exoskeleton, we proposed a torque generator with an adjustable PATA.
elements.sent-2411-232: We utilized a set of parameters (\(\alpha\), \(\beta\), \(r_{1}\), \(r_{2}\), \(r_{3}\), \(L_{i}\)) to guide the design of the torque generator, and investigated their impacts on torque profiles via geometric analysis and simulations.
elements.sent-2411-233: A subset of parameters (\(\alpha\), \(\beta\), \(L_{i}\)) can be adjusted to reconfigure the exoskeleton, each controlling peak torque, PATA, and safety limit angle, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-234: Notably, \(\beta\) can be adjusted within a range of -30º to 30º, resulting in a range of PATA from 90º to 150º, which meets the requirements of various overhead tasks and individuals.
elements.sent-2411-235: The experimental results demonstrated that monitored muscular activations in HIT-POSE Match condition decreased the most compared to the NO HIT-POSE condition, thus validating the effectiveness of PATA.
elements.sent-2411-236: However, the exoskeleton provided an assistive torque of around 2 N·m when lowering the arm, which may cause discomfort to users.
elements.sent-2411-237: This is inevitable in our current design and also a limitation of the current architecture.
elements.sent-2411-238: Although this is not reflected in users' subjective evaluations, it may be attributed to the small sample size and individual sensitivity.
elements.subsection-2411-016: Effects of the Exoskeleton on Muscular Activations
elements.sent-2411-239: For both tasks in HIT-POSE Match conditions, significantly reduced muscular activations were observed in agonist muscles (AD, MD, BB, PM, TR) and antagonist muscles (PD, LD) compared to NO HIT-POSE conditions.
elements.sent-2411-240: The exoskeleton provided support to the human shoulder, allowing agonist muscles to generate less force to achieve the same action, while also decreasing co-contraction of the antagonist muscles \cite{grazi2020design .}
elements.sent-2411-241: Similar results can be found in the ShoulderX \cite{pinho2022shoulder , PAEXO \cite{maurice2019evaluation , MATE \cite{pacifico2020experimental , and H-VEX \cite{hyun2019light .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-242: This phenomenon can be explained by the fact that there is a match between the PATA and the target task angle, resulting in increased assistive torque, with reductions consistent with the level of assistance \cite{grazi2020design, van2019experimental .}
elements.sent-2411-243: This also demonstrates the necessity of PATA for effective assistance.
elements.sent-2411-244: As reported by previous studies \cite{lee2002dynamic, labriola2005stability , the reduced muscular activation implies lower force, resulting in decreased compressive force on the shoulder, and thus potentially reducing biomechanical stress on the shoulder girdle.}
elements.sent-2411-245: Such positive effects suggest that long-term use of the exoskeleton has the potential to reduce the incidence of WMSDs, as reported by previous studies \cite{de2023passive , \cite{de2022occupational .}}
elements.sent-2411-246: Additionally, for screwing tasks in HIT-POSE Match conditions, the primary muscles (AD, MD, PD, BB, PM, and LD) significantly reduced their activations compared to HIT-POSE Mismatch conditions.

elements.sent-2411-276: In this study, we propose a passive occupational shoulder exoskeleton named HIT-POSE for overhead tasks, which enhances the adaptability of tasks and individuals while ensuring sufficient shoulder ROM.
elements.sent-2411-277: Firstly, we design an ergonomic shoulder structure by kinematic modeling and ROM simulations and verify through experiments.
elements.sent-2411-278: Secondly, we implement a torque generator with adjustable PATA through geometric analysis and simulations of torque profiles.
elements.sent-2411-279: Thirdly, we investigate the parameters' impacts on torque profiles to determine adjustable parameters to achieve various torque profiles with different PATA without reconstructing the exoskeleton.
elements.sent-2411-280: Finally, we conduct experimental evaluations with aspects of objective and subjective within three conditions for two tasks, to validate the effectiveness of our proposed exoskeleton.
elements.sent-2411-281: Overall, the results have shown that HIT-POSE can decrease the physical load on users and has the potential to reduce the incidence of shoulder WMSDs.
elements.sent-2411-282: In the future, we will strive to enhance the kinematic compliance of the exoskeleton to achieve a full ROM for the user.
elements.sent-2411-283: More realistic simulation algorithms of ROM will also be investigated to reduce the deviation between simulation and reality.
elements.sent-2411-284: In addition, a unique cam structure will be designed to further reduce the assistive torque at low elevation angles and improve the user acceptance and usability of the exoskeleton.

elements.sent-2411-287: However, they can only accommodate a specific task and cannot effectively balance between compactness and sufficient range of motion.
elements.sent-2411-288: Method: We proposed a novel passive occupational shoulder exoskeleton to handle various overhead tasks with different arm elevation angles and ensured a sufficient ROM while compactness.
elements.sent-2411-289: Overhead tasks, often related to extreme and awkward shoulder postures, are defined as working with hands above the shoulder height \cite{sluiter2001criteria .They have been recognized as a primary contributor to shoulder WMSDs \cite{ding2023lightweight .}}
elements.sent-2411-290: Although robots have been involved in many factory tasks, numerous complex activities that demand human cognition or operate in narrow spaces still require workers, especially in the construction industry \cite{huysamen2018evaluation .}
elements.sent-2411-291: Occupational shoulder exoskeleton (OSE) technology is an available solution to reduce the burden on the shoulder joint and the incidence of its WMSDs \cite{park2021shoulder .}
elements.sent-2411-292: OSEs can be classified into active and passive categories.
elements.sent-2411-293: However, they have larger volumes, resulting in bulkier and heavier, and are prone to malfunctions (i.e., increased maintenance costs) and collision \cite{kim2022passive .}
elements.sent-2411-294: Passive OSEs (POSEs) characterize as cheaper, lightweight design and a trade-off between effectiveness and wearability \cite{grazi2020design \cite{pacifico2022exoskeletons , thus are increasingly accepted by several manufacturers in real-application scenarios.}}
elements.sent-2411-295: They can provide arm gravity compensation during overhead works without external power sources.
elements.sent-2411-296: Overhead tasks relate to different positions, especially different elevation angles of shoulder (e.g., screwing task of the automobile manufacturing industry \cite{pinho2022shoulder, yin2020effects, kim2018assessing , aircraft squeeze riveting and sealing tasks \cite{jorgensen2022influence, jorgensen2022impact , the welding task of shipbuilding \cite{hyun2019light , order picking task \cite{de2020passive and so on).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-297: Even though POSEs have been commercialized, some pending issues still remain, thus impede the user acceptance and assistive efficiency \cite{rossini2021design .}
elements.sent-2411-298: One of the main issues is the insufficient kinematic compatibility between the exoskeleton and the user [23].
elements.sent-2411-299: POSEs can be classified into two types based on whether there is an exoskeletal shoulder structure above the human shoulder \cite{kim2022passive .}
elements.sent-2411-300: Exoskeletons without a structure above the shoulder, such as the MATE \cite{pacifico2020experimental , Exo4Work \cite{de2022occupational , and H-PULSE \cite{grazi2022kinematics, grazi2020design , avoid collisions at high elevation angles.}}}
elements.sent-2411-301: However, they generally incorporate multi-DoF structures in the back to accommodate shoulder movements, which leads to a larger volume.
elements.sent-2411-302: Another main issue of current POSEs is the task-specific or individual-specific design, corresponding to a fixed assistive torque profile and thus a fixed assistive region (i.e. the subset of ROM where the exoskeleton can provide assistance), which may not be optimal for other tasks \cite{maurice2019objective, hyun2019light .}
elements.sent-2411-303: Considering that the assistive torque profile for overhead tasks are mainly inverse U-shaped due to the need of compensating for the gravity of arm elevation and lowering, the torque profile should be adaptive to tasks and subjects, essentially characterized by the amplitude of the peak torque and the timing of peak torque (i.e. the peak assistive torque angle, PATA).
elements.sent-2411-304: Different overhead tasks correspond to different postures and PATAs, thus require different optimal torque profiles \cite{grazi2020design, pacifico2020experimental .}
elements.sent-2411-305: In addition, the same task may have different PATAs for individuals of different heights, which requires providing personalized torque profiles \cite{vazzoler2022analysis .}
elements.sent-2411-306: For example, the MATE can manually change the peak torque by a knob \cite{pacifico2020experimental , and the H-PULSE can adjust the spring length by a spindle drive coupled with a servomotor \cite{grazi2022kinematics, grazi2020design .}}
elements.sent-2411-307: The timing of the peak assistive torque ensures that the POSE can provide timely assistance when the shoulder torque reaches its peak value at some specific elevation angles of shoulder \cite{rossini2021design .}
elements.sent-2411-308: Fig. 1 depicts the torque profiles when the PATA matches or mismatches the requirement of the task in an overhead task with a target angle of 120º.
elements.sent-2411-309: When the PATA matches the target angle of the task, it ensures timely assistance and reduces energy loss, making it a more efficient assistive strategy.
elements.sent-2411-310: However, current POSEs can only change the PATA through reconstruction, which means they cannot provide torque profiles for different tasks or individuals, i.e., insufficient adaptability \cite{hyun2019light, vazzoler2022analysis .}
elements.sent-2411-311: In summary, different tasks and individuals should be matched with different PATA, lacking in current POSEs.
elements.sent-2411-312: Therefore, a POSE with adjustable PATA is needed to enhance its task and subject adaptability.
elements.sent-2411-313: Inspired by the issues mentioned above, the object of this study is to develop, implement, and preliminarily evaluate a novel POSE for overhead tasks, named HIT-POSE.
elements.sent-2411-314: Our prototype can provide sufficient ROM of the human shoulder and possess adjustable PATA, thus adapting to various overhead tasks and individuals.
elements.sent-2411-315: The major contributions of our work are as follows: (a) We designed a torque generator with adjustable PATA to adapt to different overhead tasks and individuals.

elements.sent-2411-321: The HIT-POSE is designed to compensate for shoulder gravitational torque while providing sufficient ROM for the shoulder, as illustrated in Fig. 2.

elements.sent-2411-324: The guiding principles of design are safety, compactness, efficiency, adequate ROM, and wearability.
elements.sent-2411-325: HIT-POSE can adjust the PATA and the peak torque to generate more versatile and efficient torque profiles, which can effectively assist the shoulder in multiple overhead tasks with different levels of arm elevations.
elements.sent-2411-326: In this subsection, we conducted kinematic modeling of the exoskeleton and the user and simulated ROM to select optimal design parameters, and therefore ensure sufficient ROM for the shoulder when wearing the exoskeleton.

elements.sent-2411-329: This phenomenon introduces great design inspiration to us.

elements.sent-2411-373: In this study, we propose a novel passive occupational shoulder exoskeleton with adjustable PATA for different overhead tasks.
elements.sent-2411-374: We first designed an ergonomic shoulder structure through kinematic modeling and ROM simulations to ensure a sufficient shoulder ROM.
elements.sent-2411-375: Then we implemented a torque generator with pulleys moved along the guide rail, thus realizing adjustable PATA.
elements.sent-2411-376: Finally, we also investigated parameters' impact on torque profiles, providing users with valuable recommendations for achieving various torque profiles through the considerable reconfiguration flexibility of the exoskeleton to adapt to diverse tasks and individuals.
elements.sent-2411-377: The HIT-POSE is equipped with an ergonomic shoulder structure to ensure sufficient ROM for the user.
elements.sent-2411-378: Typically, some passive shoulder exoskeletons integrate a multi-DoF chain or simplified two-link hinge to increase the ROM, such as MATE \cite{pacifico2023evaluation and PAEXO \cite{schmalz2019biomechanical .}}
elements.sent-2411-379: Recently, a similar study has proposed a shoulder structure to assist overhead tasks in the shipbuilding industry \cite{kim2022passive .}
elements.sent-2411-380: However, the ROM of the horizontal F/E is limited, and the design parameters are fixed, thus lacking reconfigurability.
elements.sent-2411-381: A suitable set of parameters (\(\phi\)=15°, \(d_{v}\)=80mm, \(d_{b}\)=10mm) was selected through kinematic modeling and ROM simulations.Sufficient ROM was guaranteed in both sagittal and horizontal F/E for performing overhead tasks and natural movements, as shown in Table \ref{5 , while the structure with a relatively small volume is designed to reduce the possibility of collision.}
elements.sent-2411-382: Different overhead tasks usually have different PATAs and thus different optimal torque profiles \cite{maurice2019objective, hyun2019light .}
elements.sent-2411-383: Additionally, when handling the same task, individuals with different heights may have different target angles, i.e., different tasks.
elements.sent-2411-384: Consequently, the task and individual adaptability of the exoskeleton are crucial for users, which essentially involves adjustable assistive profiles, including peak torque and PATA.
elements.sent-2411-385: To provide a comprehensive overview of the effects of the HIT-POSE on muscular activations, we monitored and analyzed eight muscles primarily involved in shoulder movement and stability in three conditions (i.e., NO HIT-POSE, HIT-POSE Match, and HIT-POSE Mismatch) during screwing at low and high heights.
elements.sent-2411-386: Overall, our findings indicated that the HIT-POSE can significantly reduce muscular activations of related muscles, thus potentially mitigating the incidence of shoulder WMSDs.
elements.sent-2411-387: When wearing the exoskeleton in both tasks, significantly reduced muscular activations were observed in agonist muscles (AD, MD, BB, TR) and antagonist muscles (PD).
elements.sent-2411-388: Similar results can be found in the ShoulderX \cite{van2018evaluation , PAEXO \cite{maurice2019evaluation , MATE \cite{pacifico2020experimental , and H-VEX \cite{hyun2019light .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-389: This observation reflects the rational and effective design of the device.
elements.sent-2411-390: As reported by a previous study \cite{lee2002dynamic , the reduced muscular activation implies lower force, resulting in decreased compressive force on the shoulder, and thus potentially reducing biomechanical stress on the shoulder girdle.}
elements.sent-2411-391: Such positive effects suggest that long-term use of the exoskeleton has the potential to reduce the incidence of WMSDs, as reported by previous studies \cite{de2023passive, de2022occupational .}
elements.sent-2411-392: Additionally, for both tasks, PM (agonist muscle) and LD (antagonist muscle) demonstrated significantly reduced muscular activations in the HIT-POSE Match condition, but not in the HIT-POSE Mismatch condition.
elements.sent-2411-393: The difference is likely due to the fact that less assistance was provided in the HIT-POSE Mismatch condition, or individuals adopted different shoulder postures for different tasks as well as conditions.
elements.sent-2411-394: Nevertheless, the more accurate explanations remain to be explored further.
elements.sent-2411-395: Notably, a reduction in the ES muscle was also expected when wearing the HIT-POSE in both tasks.
elements.sent-2411-396: The result is consistent with the existing study \cite{jakobsen2023biomechanical .}
elements.sent-2411-397: This may be attributed to the fact that the exoskeleton transmits forces to the waist through the device's back bar and waist belt.
elements.sent-2411-398: The result could indicate that the HIT-POSE doesn’t increase the risk of back WMSDs.
elements.sent-2411-399: As expected, for both tasks, all muscular activations decreased the most in the HIT-POSE Match condition.
elements.sent-2411-400: This phenomenon can be explained by the fact that matching the PATA with the target task angle results in increased assistive torque, with reductions consistent with the level of assistance \cite{grazi2020design .}
elements.sent-2411-401: In this study, we also investigated the user experience with the exoskeleton, by quantifying the changes in RPE, RPD, and PW among conditions.
elements.sent-2411-402: The physical demand and effort items in the NASA-TLX and RPE among three conditions presented similar phenomenon as the EMG results.
elements.sent-2411-403: In the HIT-POSE Match condition, they displayed statistical significance from the other two conditions, and the participants perceived the greatest assistance and thus exerted the lowest physical effort.
elements.sent-2411-404: The outcomes are in line with the previous study \cite{grazi2020design .}
elements.sent-2411-405: In addition, scores of the frustration item in NASA-TLX also exhibited a significant reduction with HIT-POSE conditions, which is in line with the existing study \cite{grazi2020design .}
elements.sent-2411-406: We speculate that mental confidence bridges the frustration from the task, which may be related to a placebo effect.
